name: Cream Puff Paste (Pata a Choux)
recipeIngredient: 1/2 cup sweet, unsalted butter, cut up into pieces
4 eggs + 1 egg beaten with a drop or two of water to make an egg wash or glaze
recipeInstructions:
1. Put water, butter (or salt) in a large heavy saucepan and bring to a boil.
2. Remove from heat (butter should be completely melted) and add the flour all at once. Mix quickly with a sturdy wooden spoon. This mixture is known as a "panade".
3. Return the saucepan to the stove over the lowest possible heat. Continue to stir with the wooden spoon while the panade dries out. This will take about 5 minutes, and a thin crust will form on the bottom of the pan.
4. The dough should be soft but not stick to your hand when you pinch it between thumb and forefinger.
5. Dump the panade into a bowl and let cool for at least 5 minutes.
6. Add the eggs, one at a time, beating well after each addition. The mixture should be smooth and creamy looking before adding the next egg.
7. After all 4 eggs have been beaten in, the dough will be shiny and satiny yellow, thick and heavy.
8. Butter and flour a cookie sheet.
9. Fill a pastry bag with the dough. If you don't have a pastry bag, use a tablespoon to drop dollops of dough onto the cookie sheet for eclairs and/or cream puffs, but a pastry bag is needed for cream puff rings.
10. For cream puffs, squeeze out balls about the size of ping pong or golf balls.
11. For eclairs, squeeze out oblongs at 3 to 4 inches long.
12. Brush the tops of the pastries with beaten egg using the bristles of the brush to gently push down and flatten any curlicues left by the pastry bag.
13. Drag the tines of a dinner fork down the length of each eclair. The resulting stripes will make for a more attractive and professional-looking product.
14. For a cream puff ring, mark a 10-inch circle on the surface of the buttered and floured cookie sheet.
15. Using a pastry bag, squeeze out a 1-inch wide "halo" of cream puff paste right on top of the circle outlined. Squeeze another 1-inch wide ring of dough adjacent to and touching the first. Squeeze a third ring directly over the crack between the other two.
16. Brush the whole thing with beaten egg and sprinkle a handful of thinly sliced almonds all over the top.
17. Let the cream puffs, eclairs, or cream puff ring sit and "dry" for no less than 15 minutes and no more than 25 minutes before baking.
18. Preheat oven to 375°F for cream puffs and/or eclairs, and to 400°F for a cream puff ring.
19. Bake the former at 375°F for about 30 minutes, or until they have puffed up nicely and turned golden brown. Bake the latter at 400°F for about 45 minutes, or until well puffed and golden brown.
20. Turn off the oven and open the door halfway. Leave it open to allow steam to escape and let pastries cool slowly and "dry" so they won't collapse or become soft and soggy.
21. After an hour has passed, remove the pastries from the oven. They are now ready to be filled.
22. To fill individual pastries: Cut cream puffs and eclairs in half (from side to side, not top to bottom). Fill with desired filling. Replace lids. Glaze with icing or pour sauce on top. Serve soon after preparation to avoid sogginess.
23. To fill a cream ring: Use a slicing knife with a long serrated blade to cut the top off the cream puff ring. Fill the bottom with praline cream (or coffee cream, or chocolate cream) filling. Fill a pastry bag with sweetened whipped cream and use a nozzle with a zigzag edge to squeeze out fancy puff-balls of whipped cream over the layer of praline cream filling.
24. Finally, replace the lid and dust the top with powdered confectioners' sugar. Keep the cream puff ring in a cool dry place until serving time.
